title: CATL's Third-Gen Shenxing LFP Battery Charges 10%–98% in Under 7 Minutes
detail: Coverage widened on April 23 for CATL's Tech Day announcement (held April 21) of its third-generation Shenxing LFP ultra-fast-charging battery. The cell achieves a full recharge from 10% to 98% in 6 minutes and 27 seconds — outpacing BYD's latest Blade Battery 2.0 (which targets 9 minutes). CATL reported a peak charge rate equivalent to 10C–15C and volumetric energy density of 600 Wh/L. The Shenxing 3.0 uses a nano-crystalline LFP cathode and an upgraded multi-gradient electrolyte architecture that reduces charge resistance at the anode-cathode interface. This chemistry retains all of LFP's inherent thermal stability and cost advantages while matching NMC-class charge speeds for the first time in a mass-production roadmap product.

media.caption: CATL third-generation Shenxing LFP battery capable of 10%–98% charge in under 7 minutes

title: ABB E-mobility Launches OM M-Series Modular Charging System Scaling to 1.2 MW
detail: ABB E-mobility officially launched the OM M-Series, a modular air-cooled DC fast-charging platform that scales from 200 kW to 1.2 MW across 36 site configurations. The system supports CCS1, CCS2, NACS, and MCS (Megawatt Charging Standard for heavy trucks) from a single architecture, making it one of the first commercial platforms to span passenger EVs and Class 8 heavy-duty freight. Power cabinets are physically separated from dispenser heads, enabling dynamic power sharing across up to 24 charge points without requiring site electrical redesign. ABB framed the launch around optimizing cost-per-kWh delivered rather than peak nameplate power, positioning the system for fleet depots, highway corridors, and freight logistics hubs.

media.caption: ABB OM M-Series modular DC charging system scaling from 200 kW to 1.2 MW

title: Australia's Grid Battery Pipeline Reaches 67.3 GW — 62% Annual Surge
detail: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator) data published April 23 showed the National Electricity Market (NEM) battery storage pipeline grew 62% year-on-year to a battery-specific 33.2 GW within an overall 67.3 GW interconnection queue — up 33% from 50.5 GW a year prior. Battery projects now represent 49% of total NEM pipeline capacity, with 74% of battery projects specifying grid-forming inverters, enabling them to independently stabilize grid frequency without synchronous generation. The pipeline nearly matches Australia's total existing ~73 GW installed grid capacity, underlining how rapidly utility-scale storage has displaced new fossil-fuel generation proposals in the country's energy planning horizon.

media.caption: Australia's NEM battery storage pipeline hits 67.3 GW — 62% year-on-year growth driven by grid-forming inverter projects
